request.question: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?

title: Pippa Passes, Kentucky
Saluta Rocks
10 Downing Street
Uriconian
On the Rocks (play)
Snag Rocks
Ramp Rocks
Recreation room
Battle of Muhu
Drawing room play
sentences: Pippa Passes is a home rule-class city located along Caney Creek in Knott County, eastern Kentucky, United States.
Its formal name was chosen to honor benefactors of Alice Lloyd College.
After more than a century, its residents commonly call the community as Caney or Caney Creek.
The population was 533 at the time of the year 2010 U.S. Census.
The small city is located in the mountainous Appalachia region, an area of coal mining.
Saluta Rocks ( ) is a group of rocks 1 nautical mile (1.9 km) east of Laurie Point, lying off the south coast and near the west end of South Georgia.
The name Mutt and Jeff was probably given by Lieutenant Commander J.M. Chaplin of the 1926.
The SGS, 1955–56, reported that the name is misleading; there are not two rocks as implied, but a group.
The rocks were renamed by the United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) for the Saluta, a transport of the South Georgia Whaling Co.
for many years.

10 Downing Street, colloquially known in the United Kingdom as Number 10, is the headquarters of the Government of the United Kingdom and the official residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury, a post which, for much of the 18th and 19th centuries and invariably since 1905, has been held by the Prime Minister.
The Uriconian rocks of Shropshire (Wrekin Terrane) are thought to be potentially related to the Longmyndian Supergroup of the Stretton Hills, Shropshire, United Kingdom.
Current geological profiling of the terranes suggests that the Uriconian rocks are of Precambrian age (Neoproterozoic Phases 2 and 3).
The Uriconian Rocks outcrop to the southeast of the Long Mynd area of the Welsh Borderland Fault System and beyond the Church Stretton Fault which trends northeast-southwest across the area.
The Stretton Hills are composed primarily of arenaceous (quartz rich sand) beds assigned to the Longmyndian Supergroup.
The Longmyndian rocks are a c.6 km thick group of volcaniclastic and bentonitic sediment horizons.

The Uriconian have long thought to be older than the Longmyndian and generally outcrop to the southeast of the latter within northeast-southwest trending lineaments suggesting basement influence for the regional structure.
On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.
The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.
It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.
Snag Rocks, ( ) also known as Rocas Bravo, is a cluster of rocks off the west coast of the Antarctic Peninsula.
This cluster of rocks lies mid-channel in the French Passage between the Roca Islands and the Myriad Islands, in the Wilhelm Archipelago.

Photographed from the helicopter of HMS Protector in March 1958, Snag Rocks was so named by United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) because the feature presents a hazard or obstacle to navigation.
Ramp Rocks ( ) are three barren rocks, the largest being 23 m high, lying 2.5 nautical miles (4.6 km) northwest of Johannesen Point, Main Island, at the west extremity of South Georgia.
The name "Laavebrua," a descriptive Norwegian term meaning literally "threshing floor bridge" or "barn bridge," was used for the largest rock by whalers and sealers at South Georgia.
In Norwegian barns used for storing hay, there is a ramp up which the wagons are driven before tipping.
"Laavebrua," which is not strictly translatable, is this ramp.
The United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) recommended in 1954 that "Ramp Rock" be approved for the large rock, but in 1976 altered the name to Ramp Rocks to include the three rocks.
The name Låvebrua Island is already approved for an island off Deception Island.
A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.
The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.
Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.
Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.
In January 1227, when the sea had frozen hard, a large Crusader army after the call of the Papal legate William of Modena was gathered.
According to the Chronicle of Livonia, 20 000 troops had set out to Muhu from the estuary of Pärnu River and moved along the ice.
Since the ice was said to have been very slippery, they reached the stronghold of Muhu on the ninth day.
After seeing such a large force, the people of Muhu offered truce and allowed themselves to be baptised.
Most of the Germans didn't accept it and so they started besieging the stronghold.
The first rush was fend off with rocks and spears.
The besiegers threw rocks into the stronghold with bricoles, built a turret and undermined the wall.

A drawing room play is a type of play, developed during the Victorian period in the United Kingdom, in which the actions take place in a drawing room or which is designed to be reenacted in the drawing room of a home.

The common practice of entertaining a guest in the home led to the creation of this category of plays and while the drawing room itself has fallen out of favour, the play format has continued to provide a source of entertainment.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?
hop1_docs: Eze-Igwe | The Oguta kingdom was established in like manners to the Benin Kingdom of Nigeria.  Many people in Oguta believe that the Oguta people migrated from the ancient Benin Kingdom.  They argue that the administrative set up, titles and common names have similarities to those of Bini people.  Although the people of Oguta do not call their king Oba as the Bini do, both kingdoms have some striking parallels.  The Binis have Iyase, in Oguta it is called Iyasara, there is Ogine among the Bini and Ogana in Oguta.  Palm oil and plantain are called ofigbo and ipa in both Edo language and Oguta dialect to this day.  Although the Oguta people speak a dialect of the Igbo language, some of their words are unmistakably of Bini origin.

Recreation room | A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.  The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.  Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.  Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.

Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song) | "Do What You Do" is a single by American R&B singer Jermaine Jackson, sibling of singers Michael and Janet Jackson and former member of The Jackson 5.  It was released as the second single his album, which was called "Jermaine Jackson" in the United States and "Dynamite" in the United Kingdom and other countries.
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do | "Hey, Hey, What Can I Do" is a song by the English rock group Led Zeppelin, released in 1970 as the B-side of "Immigrant Song" outside the United Kingdom.

On the Rocks (play) | On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.  The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.  It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.

That's What People Do | That's What People Do is an independently released album by the rock band Thousand Foot Krutch.  Rhyme Animal is the first and only single from this album, released in Canada.  The tracks titled "Rhyme Animal", "Small Town", "Lift It" and "Set It Off" were rerecorded in 2000 for the album Set It Off and "Rhyme Animal" was re-titled "Rhime Animal".  This is the only TFK album to have no music videos, until the 2009 record "Welcome to the Masquerade".  Trevor McNevan is the only member on the album who is still in the band.

Big Tent Revival | Big Tent Revival is a Christian rock band that formed in 1991, toured extensively, disbanded in 2000, and reformed in 2012.  They were featured at the Harvest Crusades.  Their most popular songs were "Two Sets of Jones'", "Choose Life", and "What Would Jesus Do?" .  The first told a story about two different couples in which one trusted in Jesus through the storms of life and the other didn't.  The second was used as an invitational at Harvest Crusade altar calls.  The last was part of the WWJD movement that encouraged people to consider what Jesus would do in real-life situations.
hop1_titles: Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song)
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do
response.summary_1: Passage evidence: "the entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street." Missing/bridge: the passages do not state what people in the United Kingdom commonly call 10 Downing Street (or its Cabinet Room). Next retrieval should look up the common UK name(s)/nickname(s) for 10 Downing Street (search for phrases like "10 Downing Street commonly called," "Number 10," or "No. 10" in UK usage).
response.hop2_query: 10 Downing Street commonly called Number 10 No. 10 UK
hop2_docs: List of residents of 10 Downing Street | Number 10 Downing Street is the residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.  The headquarters of Her Majesty's Government, it is situated on Downing Street in the City of Westminster in London, England.

10 Downing Street | 10 Downing Street, colloquially known in the United Kingdom as Number 10, is the headquarters of the Government of the United Kingdom and the official residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury, a post which, for much of the 18th and 19th centuries and invariably since 1905, has been held by the Prime Minister.

Number 10 (TV series) | Number 10 was a 1983 British television series originally aired on ITV and lasting for 7 episodes from 13 February to 27 March 1983.  It depicted the personal and political lives of seven British Prime Ministers, ranging from the 1780s to the 1920s, during their occupancy of 10 Downing Street.

Number 10 Policy Unit | The Number 10 Policy Unit is a body of policymakers in 10 Downing Street in the British government.  Originally set up to support Harold Wilson in 1974, it has gone through a series of guises to suit the needs of successive Prime Ministers, staffed variously by political advisers, civil servants or a combination of both.

11 Downing Street | 11 Downing Street (sometimes referred to as just Number 11) is the official residence of Britain's Chancellor of the Exchequer (who traditionally also has the title of Second Lord of the Treasury).  The residence, in Downing Street in London, was built alongside the official residence of the Prime Minister at Number 10 in 1682.

Downing Street mortar attack | The Downing Street mortar attack was carried out by the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) on 7 February 1991.  The IRA launched homemade mortar shells at 10 Downing Street, London, the official residence of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.  It was an attempt to assassinate Prime Minister John Major and his War Cabinet, who were meeting to discuss the Gulf War.  One of the heavy mortar shells exploded in the back garden of number 10, only yards from the cabinet office.  Due to the bomb-proof windows, none of the cabinet were hurt, though four other people received minor injuries, including two police officers.  The other two shells overshot Downing Street and landed on a green nearby.

10 Downing Street Guard Chairs | The 10 Downing Street Guard Chairs are two antique chairs.  In the early 19th century, 10 Downing Street was guarded by two men who sat outside the building in leather chairs made by Thomas Chippendale.  There was a drawer underneath the chair which was filled with hot coals to keep the guards warm whilst on duty.  The chairs were made with a circular back and hood, both to shield the guards from bad weather and to improve the surrounding acoustics, thereby allowing the guard to hear better from a wider angle.
hop2_titles: List of residents of 10 Downing Street
response.summary_2: The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street. In the UK 10 Downing Street is commonly called "Number 10" (see passage [2]).
